flows.title: User Sends a Natural Language Prompt in the Interactive REPL
flows.description: The most fundamental flow: a developer types a natural language prompt in the terminal, and the system parses it, sends it to Claude, streams the response with tool calls, and renders the result — the core conversation loop that drives all interactive usage.

participants.name: Screens
participants.description: REPL screen that captures user input and orchestrates the conversation UI

participants.name: QueryEngine
participants.description: Core AI conversation loop managing the full query lifecycle

participants.name: QueryLoop
participants.description: The streaming query generator that sends messages to Claude and processes responses

participants.name: APIClient
participants.description: Claude API client handling streaming communication with the Anthropic API

participants.name: ToolExecution
participants.description: Tool orchestration runtime that dispatches and executes tool calls in parallel

participants.name: ToolSystem
participants.description: Tool registry providing 40+ built-in tools (file ops, bash, web, etc.)

participants.name: Components
participants.description: React components rendering assistant messages, tool results, and diffs

steps.detail: The REPL screen captures the user's text input and calls QueryEngine.submitMessage() to begin a new conversation turn
submitMessage() builds the system prompt (injecting CLAUDE.md, git status, memory context), assembles the message array, and invokes the query() async generator
The query loop sends the full message array to the Anthropic streaming API, receiving incremental text and tool_use blocks

steps.action: Dispatch tool calls from Claude's response
steps.detail: When Claude's response contains tool_use blocks, the query loop passes them to runTools() for parallel execution with permission checks
The orchestrator invokes each tool's call() method (e.g., FileReadTool, BashTool, GrepTool), collecting results and streaming progress
Assistant text tokens, tool results, and status updates are yielded from the query generator and flow to the React component tree for real-time rendering
If tool results need further processing, the query loop appends tool results to messages and sends another API request, iterating until Claude emits end_turn

flows.title: CLI Startup and Initialization to REPL Launch
flows.description: When a user runs `claude` in the terminal, the application bootstraps through multiple phases — argument parsing, environment validation, auth, telemetry, migrations, MCP connections, and plugin loading — before launching the interactive REPL session.

participants.name: Entrypoints
participants.description: The outermost CLI entrypoint that dispatches to the correct execution path

participants.name: Initialization
participants.description: The init() function bootstrapping environment, networking, and telemetry

participants.name: CLIOrchestrator
participants.description: main.tsx: Commander.js program with argument parsing and full startup sequence

participants.name: EnvironmentSetup
participants.description: setup() configuring cwd, git root, worktrees, session state

participants.name: Infrastructure
participants.description: Auth, config, settings, and permission system

participants.name: MCPClient
participants.description: MCP server connections and tool discovery

participants.name: TerminalUI
participants.description: Ink-based REPL interface rendering

steps.detail: cli.tsx detects this is not a fast-path (--version, --daemon) and dynamically imports init() to set up configs, telemetry, proxy agents, and OAuth
After init(), cli.tsx dynamically imports main.tsx which defines the Commander.js program and parses CLI arguments
main.tsx validates authentication credentials (API key, OAuth token, or AWS), loads GrowthBook feature flags, shows the trust dialog, and runs migrations
Calls setup() to detect git root, set project directory, initialize session memory, start file watchers, and configure hooks
Assembles MCP server configurations from settings, connects to each server, discovers available tools, and merges them into the tool pool
After all initialization completes, calls launchRepl() which mounts the Ink React app and renders the REPL screen, ready for user input

flows.title: Automatic Context Compaction During Long Conversations
flows.description: When a conversation grows too large for the context window, the system automatically detects the overflow, summarizes older messages via an LLM call, extracts session memories, and replaces the history with a compact summary — allowing the conversation to continue seamlessly.

participants.name: QueryLoop
participants.description: Core query loop that monitors token usage and triggers compaction

participants.name: CostTracker
participants.description: Tracks token usage across the session

participants.name: Compaction
participants.description: Context compaction engine with multiple strategies (auto, micro, reactive)

participants.name: APIClient
participants.description: Sends compaction summarization requests to Claude

participants.name: SessionMemory
participants.description: Extracts and persists key information before messages are discarded

participants.name: Components
participants.description: UI components showing compaction warnings and progress

steps.detail: After each API response, the query loop checks accumulated token usage against the model's context window limits via autoCompactTracking state
When tokens approach the threshold, a warning is surfaced in the UI informing the user that auto-compaction will occur
When the token budget is exceeded, the query loop invokes the compaction engine which groups older messages into compactable segments
Before discarding old messages, the compaction engine triggers session memory extraction to persist important context (task state, decisions, file references) to disk
Sends the grouped old messages to Claude with a compaction prompt, receiving a condensed summary that preserves key technical details
The compacted summary replaces the old messages in the conversation array, and post-compact cleanup removes stale tool results, freeing token budget for continued conversation

flows.title: Subagent Spawning for Parallel Task Execution
flows.description: When Claude decides a task benefits from parallel exploration (e.g., searching a codebase while editing another file), it spawns subagents — forked processes with their own query loops that execute independently and return results to the parent conversation.

participants.name: QueryLoop
participants.description: Parent query loop processing Claude's response with Agent tool calls

participants.name: AgentAndOrchestration
participants.description: AgentTool managing subagent lifecycle, forking, and built-in agent types

participants.name: ToolExecution
participants.description: Tool orchestration dispatching the AgentTool call

participants.name: Infrastructure
participants.description: Permission system gating agent tool access

participants.name: APIClient
participants.description: Claude API client used by the subagent's own query loop

participants.name: Components
participants.description: Agent progress display and color-coded output in the terminal

steps.detail: Claude's response contains a tool_use block for the Agent tool with a prompt and agent type (e.g., 'Explore', 'Plan'). The query loop passes it to runTools()
The tool orchestrator calls AgentTool.call() which selects the agent type, prepares the prompt with agent-specific system instructions, and configures the execution context
The agent tool verifies permission to spawn subagents, resolves worktree isolation if requested, and applies tool deny rules for the agent's restricted tool set

steps.action: Fork and run subagent query loop
steps.detail: A forked subagent process starts its own query loop, sending the agent prompt to Claude via a separate API stream. The subagent has its own tool set (e.g., Explore agents get read-only tools)
The subagent's activity (tool calls, intermediate results) is displayed in the terminal with color-coded labels distinguishing it from the parent conversation
When the subagent completes, its final result is returned as a tool_result to the parent query loop, which incorporates it into the ongoing conversation

flows.title: claude.ai Bridge: Remote-Controlled Session via WebSocket
flows.description: A user on claude.ai initiates a Claude Code session that connects to a local or cloud-hosted CLI instance via WebSocket. Messages flow from the web UI through the bridge to the local query engine, with tool permissions relayed back for approval.

participants.name: Bridge
participants.description: WebSocket bridge connecting claude.ai to local Claude Code

participants.name: CLIIOLayer
participants.description: Headless query runner (print.ts) for non-interactive execution

participants.name: QueryEngine
participants.description: Core query engine processing the remote user's prompts

participants.name: ToolSystem
participants.description: Tool registry and execution for the remote session

participants.name: Infrastructure
participants.description: Auth (JWT verification) and permission system for remote sessions

steps.detail: The bridge receives a session initiation from claude.ai, verifies JWT tokens, establishes WebSocket/SSE transport, and creates a session with work-dispatch polling

steps.action: Relay user message to headless runner
steps.detail: When a user message arrives via WebSocket, the bridge passes it to the headless query runner (print.ts runHeadless) which drives the conversation without a terminal UI
The headless runner calls into the query engine to process the message through the same API→tools→response cycle as interactive mode
Tool calls are executed locally, but permission prompts are relayed back through the bridge to the remote user for approval via permission callbacks
The headless runner serializes assistant responses, tool results, and status updates as NDJSON messages which the bridge relays over WebSocket back to the web UI

flows.title: MCP Tool Discovery and Invocation
flows.description: When Claude Code starts, it connects to configured MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers, discovers their available tools, and merges them into the tool pool. During conversation, Claude can invoke these external tools just like built-in ones.

participants.name: CLIOrchestrator
participants.description: main.tsx startup sequence that initiates MCP connections

participants.name: MCPClient
participants.description: MCP protocol client managing server connections and tool discovery

participants.name: ToolInterfaceAndRegistry
participants.description: Tool registry that assembles built-in and MCP tools into one pool

participants.name: MCPAndIntegrations
participants.description: MCPTool proxy that delegates calls to MCP server connections

participants.name: QueryLoop
participants.description: Query loop that sends MCP tools to Claude and dispatches tool calls

participants.name: Infrastructure
participants.description: Settings defining MCP server configurations and approval state

steps.detail: During startup, main.tsx reads MCP server definitions from project settings, user settings, and the official registry configuration
For each configured server, the MCP client establishes a connection (stdio, SSE, or HTTP transport), performs initialization handshake, and queries available tools/resources
Discovered MCP tools are wrapped as MCPTool proxy instances and merged into the tool pool via assembleToolPool(), becoming available to Claude alongside built-in tools
When Claude's response includes a tool_use for an MCP-provided tool, the query loop dispatches it to the MCPTool proxy which translates the call to MCP protocol format
The MCPTool proxy sends the tool invocation over the MCP transport to the external server, awaits the result, and applies result collapsing classification before returning
The MCP tool result is formatted as a standard ToolResult and fed back into the query loop's message array for Claude to process in the next turn

flows.title: End-of-Turn Memory Extraction and Persistence
flows.description: After Claude finishes responding to a user prompt, the system runs background stop hooks that automatically extract durable memories (user preferences, project context, feedback) from the conversation and persist them to the structured memory store for use in future sessions.

participants.name: StopHooks
participants.description: End-of-turn lifecycle handler running post-response hooks

participants.name: ExtractMemories
participants.description: Background agent that identifies and saves durable memories from conversation

participants.name: MemoryRetrieval
participants.description: Scans existing memories to avoid duplicates

participants.name: MemdirCore
participants.description: Memory directory infrastructure managing typed memory files

participants.name: SystemContext
participants.description: System prompt builder that injects memory context into future conversations

steps.detail: When the model emits end_turn, handleStopHooks() fires executeExtractMemories() as a background task, passing the recent conversation transcript
The extraction agent scans the memory directory for existing memory files and their frontmatter to build a manifest, ensuring it won't create duplicate entries
The extraction agent identifies durable facts (user role, feedback, project context) from the conversation and writes them as individual markdown files with typed frontmatter to the memory directory
After writing memory files, the agent updates the MEMORY.md index file with pointers to the new entries, keeping the index concise for future prompt injection
In subsequent conversations, the system prompt builder reads MEMORY.md and relevant memory files, injecting them into Claude's context so it has awareness of the user's preferences and project state
